critic_raw:
The experiment predicts equipment failure from sensor data 'collected every second from over 100 fabrication tools across 24 months' using a stratified random split. At 1-second resolution, each tool generates roughly 86,400 observations per day. A random split of individual second-resolution samples from the same tool across train and test creates massive temporal leakage: the model observes a tool's sensor readings at second t in training and is tested on readings at second t+1 from the same tool, in the same failure regime. The high autocorrelation of second-resolution sensor data means test performance primarily reflects the model's ability to memorize recent sensor states rather than detect early failure precursors across new failure events.

The F1-score as the primary metric for equipment failure prediction requires choosing a decision threshold. At second-level resolution, the positive class frequency determines the denominator of recall, but a single failure event spanning hours produces thousands of consecutive positive-labeled seconds. This creates a definition ambiguity: is a 'hit' a correct prediction for any second in a failure window, or does the model need to provide early warning before the failure window begins? If the latter, F1 computed on second-level labels dramatically overstates operational performance (any model that predicts 'failure' during the failure window gets credit for thousands of true positives).

Stratification is 'based on production shifts and maintenance schedules' as a control for confounds. But semiconductor tool failures are correlated with specific process recipes and wafer lots — a tool running recipe A has systematically different failure patterns than the same tool running recipe B. If recipe is not part of the stratification, the confound remains uncontrolled and the model learns recipe-failure correlations rather than tool degradation patterns.

The logistic regression baseline with 'engineered time-series features' is appropriate, but those features are not specified. Time-series features for sensor data span a wide range (rolling statistics, spectral features, wavelet coefficients, threshold crossing counts). Without specifying which aggregation window and feature types are used, the baseline's quality is unknown and the comparison may be testing feature representation choices rather than model architecture.

Class imbalance handling is noted (stratified split for minority class) but no oversampling, cost-sensitive learning, or positive-weight adjustment strategy is specified for the LSTM training. In highly imbalanced settings (semiconductor failures are rare — typically 0.1–1% of observations), the default LSTM training with cross-entropy loss will suppress failure prediction, and a stratified split alone does not address training-time class imbalance.
all_issues_raised: Second-resolution stratified random splitting of sensor data from the same tools creates extreme temporal autocorrelation leakage — adjacent seconds from the same tool and failure event appear in both train and test sets, so measured F1 reflects memorization of current sensor state rather than early failure detection capability.

F1 computed at second-level granularity without defining an early-warning evaluation window conflates predicting correctly during an ongoing failure event (trivial, thousands of consecutive positive labels) with detecting failure before it begins (the operationally meaningful task).

Recipe/process-type is a dominant confounder for semiconductor tool failures but is not included in the stratification strategy — the model may learn recipe-failure correlations rather than tool degradation signals.

The logistic regression baseline's engineered time-series features (aggregation window, feature types) are unspecified, making the comparison unreplicable and potentially testing feature representation quality rather than model architecture.

No training-time class imbalance correction (cost-sensitive loss, oversampling) is specified; at semiconductor failure rates (0.1–1%), default cross-entropy training will produce a model biased toward non-failure prediction that may nonetheless show adequate F1 on a balanced evaluation set.
all_issues_adjudicated: Second-resolution stratified random splitting of sensor data from the same tools creates extreme temporal autocorrelation leakage — adjacent seconds from the same tool and failure event appear in both train and test sets, so measured F1 reflects memorization of current sensor state rather than early failure detection capability.

F1 computed at second-level granularity without defining an early-warning evaluation window conflates predicting correctly during an ongoing failure event (trivial, thousands of consecutive positive labels) with detecting failure before it begins (the operationally meaningful task).

Recipe/process-type is a dominant confounder for semiconductor tool failures but is not included in the stratification strategy — the model may learn recipe-failure correlations rather than tool degradation signals.

The logistic regression baseline's engineered time-series features (aggregation window, feature types) are unspecified, making the comparison unreplicable and potentially testing feature representation quality rather than model architecture.

No training-time class imbalance correction (cost-sensitive loss, oversampling) is specified; at semiconductor failure rates (0.1–1%), default cross-entropy training will produce a model biased toward non-failure prediction that may nonetheless show adequate F1 on a balanced evaluation set.
